Title: The role of sterols in plant growth and development

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Post-squalene sterol pathway, early biosynthetic unit
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Reduced downstream sterol intermediates leading to Δ5-sterols
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the C14 double bond of sterol intermediates during post-squalene sterol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of FACKEL depletes campesterol and causes severe embryonic patterning defects, dwarfism, and reduced brassinosteroids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FACKEL was identified as sterol-14-reductase in Arabidopsis mutants. 2. Mutants accumulated Δ8,14-dienic sterol intermediates and had strongly reduced campesterol. 3. Developmental defects were not fully attributable to brassinosteroid deficiency alone, indicating essential sterol functions in embryogenesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in actively dividing and expanding tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark germination then illumination in one mutant screen; comparison with 15-azasterol treatment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; sterol profiling; molecular identification; GUS expression; brassinosteroid complementation test
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Biochemical complementation with brassinosteroids failed to restore wild type during germination, supporting a direct sterol requirement beyond BR deficiency.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Δ7-sterols such as Δ7-campesterol and Δ7-sitosterol precursors
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces the C5(6) double bond into Δ7-sterol substrates in the pathway to Δ5-sterol end products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutations cause accumulation of Δ7-sterols, severe depletion of campesterol, dwarfism, reduced fertility, and brassinosteroid deficiency-like phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ste1/dwarf7/bul1 encodes Δ7-sterol-C5(6)-desaturase. 2. Mutants accumulate Δ7-sterols up to 60–90% of total sterols. 3. Loss of detectable campesterol leads to impaired brassinosteroid synthesis and dwarf phenotypes, with only partial rescue by exogenous brassinosteroids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; sterol profiling; brassinolide complementation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Partial rescue and persistent fertility defects indicate sterol-specific roles beyond BR shortage.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Δ5-sterol end products including campesterol and sitosterol
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the Δ7 double bond of Δ5,7-sterols to produce final Δ5-sterol end products
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes strong block at the Δ7-reduction step, severe loss of campesterol and end-product sterols, and dwarf/brassinosteroid-deficient phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DWARF5 encodes Δ5,7-sterol-Δ7-reductase. 2. Mutants accumulate Δ5,7-sitosterol, Δ7-sitosterol, and Δ8-sitosterol, with campesterol reduced to about 1%. 3. Overexpression of DWARF5 rescues mutant phenotypes, supporting its causal role in sterol and brassinosteroid precursor biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Allelic analysis; sterol profiling; transgenic overexpression rescue; brassinosteroid complementation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Molecular lesions included mRNA stability defects, splice-site, nonsense, and missense mutations.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 24-methyl and 24-ethyl sterols including campesterol and sitosterol
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes Δ24 isomerization and subsequent Δ24(25) reduction on sterol side chains to generate reduced end-product sterols
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation depletes campesterol and sitosterol while accumulating 24-methylene cholesterol and isofucosterol, causing brassinosteroid deficiency-like dwarfism and reduced fertility
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DIM/DWARF1/LKB1 controls side-chain Δ24 reduction in Arabidopsis and pea. 2. Mutants accumulate non-reduced end-metabolites and show strong depletion of campesterol and sitosterol. 3. Partial rescue by exogenous brassinosteroids indicates additional sterol requirements in development and fertility.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; steroid profiling; brassinosteroid complementation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review interprets DIM/DWARF1 as bifunctional for both Δ24 isomerization and reduction.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to C24 of cycloartenol to initiate 24-alkyl sterol formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for sterol ratio
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SMT1 increases cholesterol and decreases sitosterol, causing reduced growth, poor fertility, and embryo abortion without major campesterol change
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SMT1 determines flux from cycloartenol into 24-alkyl sterols. 2. Arabidopsis smt1 mutants accumulate cholesterol at the expense of sitosterol and show reduced growth and maternal-effect fertility defects. 3. Complementation with SMT1 restores the phenotype, indicating that proper sterol ratios are critical for development.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transposon mutant analysis; reciprocal crosses; sterol profiling; complementation; transgenic antisense/cosuppression comparison in tobacco
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis but not tobacco shows an SMT1 bypass enabling cholesterol accumulation in smt1 mutants.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Plant tissues and organs including flowers, siliques, stems
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group to the side chain of 24-methylene lophenol to favor 24-ethyl sterol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for campesterol:sitosterol ratio
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases sitosterol and lowers campesterol, whereas cosuppression causes high campesterol and low sitosterol; both altered ratios affect stature and fertility
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SMT2:1 controls the balance between 24-methyl sterols and 24-ethyl sterols. 2. Overexpression shifts sterol composition toward sitosterol and produces reduced stature rescueable by 24-epibrassinolide. 3. Cosuppression causes high campesterol, altered flower morphology, branching, and very low fertility, showing the developmental importance of sterol ratios. 4. cvp1 was later identified as an SMT2;1 defect causing campesterol accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; cosuppression; sterol profiling; phenotyping; exogenous 24-epibrassinolide rescue
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In tobacco overexpressing AtSMT2:1, microsomes showed elevated SMT2 and SMT1 activities. Flowers and siliques were more responsive than stems to altered campesterol:sitosterol ratio.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as one of three Arabidopsis sterol methyltransferase genes in the pathway controlling sterol ratios
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis contains AtSMT2:2 as one of the sterol methyltransferase genes. 2. In this review, no direct mutant or transgenic nutrient-metabolism phenotype is detailed for SMT2:2.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned for pathway organization/background but not experimentally developed as a major focal gene in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Isomerizes the Δ8 double bond to a Δ7 double bond in sterol intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes severe embryonic and post-embryonic morphogenetic defects linked to altered membrane-dependent hormone perception/signaling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. hydra1 carries a defect in the Δ8-Δ7 sterol isomerase gene. 2. The mutant shows severe developmental defects. 3. The phenotype was proposed to arise from constitutive changes in hormone perception and signaling due to altered sterol composition.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in the Note added section as recent supporting evidence.
